title: Panel Feed Assembly
description: Assemble the components for feeding a residential sub-panel from the main panel in the correct order.

parts.name: Double-pole breaker in main panel
Feeder conductors (2 hots, 1 neutral, 1 ground) in conduit
Sub-panel enclosure mounted at new location
Feeder conductors terminated on sub-panel main lugs
Separate neutral and ground bus bars (no bonding jumper)

explanation: Sub-panel feed assembly starts at the source breaker, runs feeder conductors, and terminates at the sub-panel. The critical detail is that the neutral must NOT be bonded to ground at the sub-panel — only at the main service panel per NEC 250.32(B). Installing a bonding jumper at a sub-panel creates dangerous parallel return paths.

title: Receptacle Circuit Assembly
description: Assemble the components of a bedroom receptacle circuit from the panel to the last outlet in the correct order.

parts.name: 15A AFCI combination breaker in panel
14/2 NM-B cable from panel to first box
First duplex receptacle with pigtail connections
14/2 NM-B cable daisy-chained to subsequent boxes
Last receptacle in circuit (end of run)

explanation: Bedroom receptacle circuits require AFCI protection per NEC 210.12. The circuit starts at the AFCI breaker, runs a 'home run' cable to the first box, and daisy-chains to subsequent outlets. Pigtail connections (instead of using device terminals for pass-through) ensure downstream outlets stay energized if a device is removed.

title: 3-Way Switch Circuit
description: Assemble the wiring components for a 3-way switch circuit controlling a hallway light from two locations.

parts.name: Power feed (14/2 NM-B) to first 3-way switch box
First 3-way switch (common terminal to hot, travelers to brass screws)
14/3 NM-B cable (travelers) between switch boxes
Second 3-way switch (common terminal to switch leg, travelers from brass screws)
Switch leg (14/2 NM-B) from second switch to light fixture

explanation: A 3-way switch circuit uses two 3-way switches and a 3-wire cable (14/3) for the travelers between them. Power enters at one switch common terminal, the two travelers connect via the 14/3 cable, and the other switch common goes to the light. The 14/3 cable carries the two traveler wires and the neutral per NEC 404.2(C).

title: GFCI Circuit Assembly
description: Assemble the components for a GFCI-protected bathroom circuit from the panel to protected downstream outlets.

parts.name: 20A circuit breaker in panel
12/2 NM-B home run cable to first box
GFCI receptacle (LINE terminals connected to panel feed)
12/2 NM-B cable from GFCI LOAD terminals to downstream boxes
Standard receptacles downstream (protected by upstream GFCI)

distractors.name: GFCI breaker (alternative method, not used here)

explanation: GFCI devices have LINE and LOAD terminals. LINE connects to the power source (panel). LOAD feeds downstream receptacles, which are then GFCI-protected without needing individual GFCI devices. This is called 'feed-through' protection per NEC 210.8. The GFCI must be the first device in the circuit for maximum protection.

title: Motor Starter Assembly
description: Assemble the motor branch circuit components from the panel to the motor in the correct order.

parts.name: Motor branch circuit overcurrent protection (breaker/fuses)
Branch circuit conductors sized at 125% of motor FLA
Motor disconnect switch within sight of motor
Magnetic contactor with overload relay (starter)
Motor lead connections in motor terminal box

explanation: Motor circuits per NEC Article 430 have a specific hierarchy: branch circuit overcurrent protection (sized per 430.52, typically 250% of FLC for inverse-time breakers), conductors at 125% FLC (430.22), disconnect within sight (430.102), starter with overloads sized to motor FLA (430.32), and finally the motor connections.

title: Service Entrance Assembly
description: Assemble the residential service entrance components from the utility connection to the main panel in the correct order.

parts.name: Weather head (service head) at top of service mast
Service entrance cable down mast to meter socket
Meter socket (utility connection point)
Service entrance conductors from meter to main panel
200A main breaker panel with neutral-ground bond
Grounding electrode conductor to ground rod system

explanation: The residential service entrance per NEC Article 230 flows from the utility: weather head catches the service drop, cable runs to the meter socket (utility's metering point), then to the main panel where the neutral-ground bond is made. The grounding electrode system connects the entire installation to earth per NEC 250.50.

title: EMT Conduit Run Assembly
description: Assemble the components for an EMT conduit run between two junction boxes in the correct order.

parts.name: EMT connector at first junction box knockout
EMT conduit with bends (not exceeding 360 degrees total)
EMT coupling at conduit joint (if needed)
EMT connector at second junction box knockout
Conduit support straps within 3 feet of each box and every 10 feet

explanation: EMT conduit runs require proper connectors at each box (never just pushed in), bends within the 360-degree limit per NEC 358.26, couplings to join conduit lengths, and support straps per NEC 358.30 (within 3 feet of each box and every 10 feet maximum). Compression fittings are required in wet locations; set-screw fittings are acceptable in dry locations.

title: Ground Rod Connection Assembly
description: Assemble the grounding electrode system components from the panel to the earth in the correct order.

parts.name: Grounding bus bar in main panel
Grounding electrode conductor (#4 AWG copper minimum for 200A)
Listed grounding clamp or exothermic weld
First 8-foot copper-clad ground rod driven full depth
Bonding jumper to second ground rod at least 6 feet away

explanation: The grounding electrode system per NEC 250.50-250.70 provides the earth connection for the electrical system. Two ground rods are standard practice (second rod required if first exceeds 25 ohms per NEC 250.53). The GEC must be continuous or spliced only with irreversible compression connectors per NEC 250.64(C).

title: Junction Box Assembly
description: Assemble the components for making proper splice connections inside a junction box in the correct order.

parts.name: Secure NM cables to box with cable clamps (within 12 inches)
Strip cable sheath to expose individual conductors (1/4 inch inside box minimum)
Connect all grounding conductors together with green wire nut (pigtail to box if metal)
Splice neutral (white) conductors together with wire connectors
Splice hot (black) conductors together with wire connectors
Fold conductors neatly into box and install blank cover plate

explanation: Junction box connections follow a specific order for safety: secure cables first, strip insulation, then connect grounds first (so the safety ground is established), neutrals next, and hots last per best practices. The cover plate must remain accessible per NEC 314.29. Grounds are connected first as the fundamental safety principle.

title: Transfer Switch Assembly
description: Assemble the components of an automatic transfer switch installation from utility source to emergency loads.

parts.name: Utility (normal) power feed to ATS line side
Generator output feed to ATS emergency side
ATS transfer mechanism (electrically and mechanically interlocked)
ATS load side output to emergency distribution panel
Control wiring for generator start signal and status monitoring

explanation: The automatic transfer switch per NEC Article 700/701/702 has two source inputs (utility and generator), a mechanically interlocked transfer mechanism that prevents both sources from feeding the load simultaneously, and control wiring that starts the generator on utility power loss. Anti-paralleling is the most critical safety feature.

title: Lighting Circuit Assembly
description: Assemble a basic switch-controlled lighting circuit from the panel to the fixture in the correct order.

parts.name: 15A or 20A breaker in lighting panel
14/2 NM-B cable (home run) to switch box
Single-pole switch with hot wire on terminal (neutral passed through)
14/2 NM-B switch leg to ceiling box
Light fixture mounted to ceiling box with proper connections

explanation: A basic switch loop starts with power from the panel to the switch box. NEC 404.2(C) now requires a neutral wire at switch boxes for future smart switch/timer compatibility. The switch interrupts only the hot conductor — never the neutral. The fixture box must be rated to support the fixture weight per NEC 314.27.

title: Sub-Panel Feed to Detached Garage
description: Assemble the components for feeding a sub-panel in a detached garage from the main house panel.

parts.name: Double-pole breaker in house main panel
4-wire feeder (2 hots, 1 neutral, 1 ground) in underground conduit
PVC conduit buried at minimum 18 inches depth
Sub-panel with separate neutral and ground buses (no bond)
Grounding electrode system at detached structure
Disconnect means at or within the detached garage

explanation: Feeding a detached structure per NEC 225.30-225.40 and 250.32 requires a 4-wire feeder (3-wire is only allowed for existing installations), separate neutral and ground at the remote panel, a local grounding electrode system, and a disconnect means. Underground conduit burial depth is 18 inches minimum for PVC per NEC Table 300.5. The neutral must NEVER be bonded to ground at the remote panel.

title: high-rise office Office TI: Tenant Panel Feed Assembly
description: Assemble the components for feeding a new tenant panelboard (L30B) from the tenant transformer on Level 30 of the high-rise commercial office TI.

parts.name: Tenant transformer (/TR-T) with secondary overcurrent protection
Feeder conductors sized per panel schedule with VD% verification
Panel L30B enclosure mounted with 36-inch working clearance
Fully rated branch circuit breakers (no series rating allowed)
TVSS (Transient Voltage Surge Suppressor) installed at panel

explanation: Tenant panel feed assembly in the high-rise commercial starts at the tenant transformer and flows through properly sized feeders to the panel. The high-rise office specification requires fully rated breakers (series rating prohibited) and TVSS at each panel. Voltage drop (VD%) must be verified against the single-line diagram calculations to ensure the feeder-plus-branch total stays under 5%.

title: high-rise office Office TI: Low Voltage Conduit Assembly
description: Assemble the components for a wall-mounted low voltage junction box conduit run on Level 31 of the high-rise commercial office TI.

parts.name: Wall-mounted junction box at low voltage equipment location
1-inch minimum conduit connected to junction box
Conduit routed vertically up through wall into ceiling space
Conduit stub extended 6 inches above hung ceiling
Conduit stub turned towards termination closet

distractors.name: Conduit terminated flush with ceiling grid

explanation: The high-rise office low voltage conduit assembly follows a specific pathway from wall to ceiling. The 1-inch minimum conduit (not 1/2-inch) provides adequate capacity for data cables. The 6-inch stub above the hung ceiling (not flush) allows proper cable bending radius. Turning the stub towards the termination closet streamlines cable pulls and prevents cable congestion above the ceiling.

title: high-rise office Office TI: Data Room Power Assembly
description: Assemble the electrical power distribution components for a data room on Level 32 of the high-rise commercial office TI.

parts.name: Dedicated panelboard with isolated ground (IG) bus
Branch circuits with insulated equipment grounding conductors
Isolated ground (IG) receptacles (orange triangle) at server racks
UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) connected to critical loads
PDU (Power Distribution Unit) distributing power to equipment racks
Signal reference ground bar (SRG) bonded to building steel

explanation: Data room power assembly in the high-rise commercial office follows a clean-power hierarchy: dedicated panel with IG bus provides isolation from the building's general electrical system, insulated grounding conductors maintain that isolation to the receptacles, UPS provides battery backup during outages, and the PDU distributes power to individual equipment. The SRG establishes a common ground reference that prevents ground loops between interconnected data equipment per TIA-607-C.

title: high-rise office Office TI: Leak Detection System Assembly
description: Assemble the leak detection system components for a data room on Level 30 of the high-rise commercial office TI.

parts.name: Leak detection panel (LDP) mounted in accessible location
Low voltage control wiring from LDP to sensor locations
Leak detectors (LD) installed at floor level in strategic locations
Alarm relay wired from LDP to building monitoring system
System test — simulate leak at each sensor and verify alarm activation

distractors.name: Smoke detector in data room ceiling

explanation: The high-rise commercial office's leak detection system protects data room equipment from water damage. The LDP is the central controller that monitors all LD sensors. Leak detectors are placed at floor level near potential water sources — HVAC condensate drains, sprinkler risers, and plumbing penetrations. The alarm relay to the building monitoring system ensures 24/7 notification. In a high-rise, water leaks from floors above can travel through conduit penetrations and cable trays, making leak detection essential for any data room.

title: high-rise office Office TI: Title 24 Lighting Control Assembly
description: Assemble the California Title 24 compliant lighting control system for an office floor on Level 31 of the high-rise commercial office TI at downtown office tower, LA.

parts.name: Lighting panel (L31B) with 277V circuits for LED troffers
Automatic time-switch control with 7-day programmable schedule
Occupancy sensors in private offices, conference rooms, and restrooms
Daylight harvesting sensors in perimeter zone (within 15 feet of windows)
Dimming drivers in LED troffers for daylight responsive dimming
Emergency battery packs in designated fixtures on emergency circuit
Title 24 compliance forms (NRCC-ELC-E and NRCC-LTS-E) completed and filed

distractors.name: Manual dimmer switches at each fixture
Relay panel for on/off switching only

explanation: California Title 24 lighting controls for the high-rise commercial office require a layered approach: time-switch scheduling turns lights off during unoccupied hours, occupancy sensors handle individual rooms, and daylight harvesting dims perimeter lighting when natural light is sufficient. Dimming drivers in the LED fixtures enable the daylight sensors to smoothly reduce artificial light levels. Emergency battery packs provide 90-minute backup per NEC 700.12. The compliance forms (NRCC-ELC-E for electrical, NRCC-LTS-E for lighting) must be completed accurately and filed with the building department before certificate of occupancy.
